coredata.dc:title: Promoted deposition of polydopamine by carbon quantum dots to construct loose nanofiltration membranes
coredata.prism:publicationName: Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects
coredata.dc:creator: Mi, Yi-Fang
Huang, Yi-Han
He, Shu-Heng
Cao, Zhi-Hai
Shentu, Bao-Qing
coredata.dc:description: Polydopamine (PDA) coating offers an environmentally friendly and facile method to modulate membrane surface properties. However, the oxidation of dopamine process by air normally needs a long time and forms uneven PDA coatings, which limit the applications of PDA. Herein, we proposed a strategy to promote the fast deposition of PDA triggered by carbon quantum dots (CQDs) under visible light irradiation. The reactive oxygen species generated by CQDs enhance the covalent polymerization and deposition rate of dopamine, thus, forming a uniform and smooth nanofiltration membrane surface. Under the optimized conditions, the PDA coating triggered by CQDs endows the membrane with superior dye rejection of methyl blue (99.7%) and water flux of 24.4 L m-2 h-1 at 0.6 MPa. Moreover, the membrane exhibits great potential in selective separation between dye and salt, the separation factor of methyl blue /NaCl can be 199.8. This work provides an efficient strategy to construct smooth PDA coatings with a fast deposition rate, which is beneficial to tailor loose nanofiltration membrane surfaces for wide applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Polydopamine
Carbon quantum dots
Dye/salt separation
Nanofiltration membranes

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fast PDA deposition promoted by CQDs on PAN substrate
UV–vis spectra of dopamine solutions accelerated by CQDs
Estimation of ROS generated by CQDs
Accelerated oxidative polymerization of dopamine by CQDs
Deposition of PDA on PAN substrate assisted by CQDs

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21908200 ), the Natural Science Foundation of Zhejiang Province ( LY22B060011 ), and the Zhejiang Provincial Science and Technology Innovation Program (New Young Talent Program) for College Students ( 2022R406A005 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram of dopamine deposition triggered by CQDs.

(a) Photographs of original dopamine solution and dopamine solution with CQDs (2 mg mL-1) and (b) time-dependence of absorbance at 420 nm for dopamine solution with or without visible light irradiation and the mixture of dopamine and CQDs solution under visible light irradiation.
(a) Energy gap of CQDs tested by UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra; (b) ROS production by CQDs (The blank sample was the DCFH aqueous solution).
(a) FTIR spectra of NFM, CNFM-45, and CNFM-60; (b) XPS spectra of PAN substrate, CNFM-45, and CNFM-60; High-resolution C 1 s spectra of PDA coating triggered by air (c) and CQDs (d), respectively.
SEM/AFM surface morphologies of (a)/(a’) hydrolyzed PAN, (b)/(b’) NFM, and (c)/(c’) CNFM-60, respectively.
Cross-section SEM images of hydrolyzed PAN (a), NFM (b), CNFM-45 (c), and CNFM-60 (d), respectively.
Water contact angles of PAN, NFM, and CNFM-X.
(a) Pore size distributions of NFM and CNFM-60; (b) surface zeta potentials of NFM and CNFM-60, respectively.

The water flux (a) and MYB dye rejection (b) of NFM and CNFM at various deposition times (CQDs: 2 mg mL-1); The effect of DA: CQDs mass ratio on the water flux (c) and dye rejection (d) on CNFM (deposition time:60 min).

(a) Effect of NaCl concentrations on separation performance of CNFM-60 (test conditions: MYB 0.1 g L-1, at 25 oC, 0.6 MPa.). (b) Variation of water flux of CNFM-60 during cyclic fouling test with 0.1 g L-1 MYB+ 1 g L-1 NaCl at 25 oC, 0.6 MPa; (c) Long-term stability of CNFM-60 (tested with 0.1 g L-1 MYB aqueous solution).
Properties of dyes used in this manuscript.

head.ce:title: Promoted deposition of polydopamine by carbon quantum dots to construct loose nanofiltration membranes

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Lab of Chemical Engineering, College of Chemical and Biological Engineering, Zhejiang University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Polydopamine (PDA) coating offers an environmentally friendly and facile method to modulate membrane surface properties. However, the oxidation of dopamine process by air normally needs a long time and forms uneven PDA coatings, which limit the applications of PDA. Herein, we proposed a strategy to promote the fast deposition of PDA triggered by carbon quantum dots (CQDs) under visible light irradiation. The reactive oxygen species generated by CQDs enhance the covalent polymerization and deposition rate of dopamine, thus, forming a uniform and smooth nanofiltration membrane surface. Under the optimized conditions, the PDA coating triggered by CQDs endows the membrane with superior dye rejection of methyl blue (99.7%) and water flux of 24.4 L m-2 h-1 at 0.6 MPa. Moreover, the membrane exhibits great potential in selective separation between dye and salt, the separation factor of methyl blue /NaCl can be 199.8. This work provides an efficient strategy to construct smooth PDA coatings with a fast deposition rate, which is beneficial to tailor loose nanofiltration membrane surfaces for wide applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Every year, the textile industries discharge a large amount of highly-saline textile wastewater, which will cause a serious environmental threat to human beings [1]. The salts are detrimental to the biodegradation treatment of textile wastewater, while salts are regarded as a renewable resource [2]. Therefore, effective separation of dye/salt mixture is of great significance in textile wastewater treatment. Nanofiltration (NF) is regarded as a promising technique to produce clean water due to its potential for high efficiency, low cost, as well as easy integration and modularity [3,4]. However, the mainstream NF membranes possess a dense selective layer, and can almost entirely reject both dye molecules and multivalent salts. To make up for the deficiency of mainstream NF in the selective separation of organic matter and inorganic salt, loose NF membranes with a relatively large pore size are developed as a potential candidate in resource recovery from natural products and wastewater, because of their desirable performances of high salt permeation and organic molecules rejection [5]. Substantial strategies are proposed to construct loose NF membranes, including interfacial polymerization [6], phase inversion [7], incorporation of nanomaterials [8], and so on.

The separation performances of membranes are closely connected to the physicochemical properties of surfaces [9]. Thus, strategies including surface graft polymerization [10], dipping coating [11], and layer-by-layer assembly [12], have been proposed to effectively tune the properties of NF membrane surfaces. Although separation purposes are realized, most surface modification techniques remain the restrictions, for instance, complicated processes, poor universality, and weak stability, to be addressed.

Polydopamine (PDA) has been regarded as a potential candidate for membrane surface engineering by virtue of its facile and versatile characteristics [13,14]. Through oxidative polymerization under alkaline conditions, the PDA layer is formed and can deposit to various surfaces without structural damage [15]. Moreover, functional groups such as amine and catechol derived from PDA enable facile subsequent functionalization of the surface through covalently linking molecules or chelating metal ions [16], endowing the modified NF membranes with extra superior features. Numerous studies on surface engineering with PDA coatings confirmed an overall improvement in membrane performance [17,18]. Despite numerous advantages of PDA coating, long deposition time (hours up to a few days) and aggregation into nanoparticles still exist as challenges that seriously influence their separation performance [19]. Typically, the generation of PDA is a spontaneous process that lacks the controlled growth of PDA coating due to the competition between polymerization on the surface and aggregation into nanoparticles. The low oxidation degree of dopamine due to the oxygen diffusion barrier is responsible for this time-consuming process [20]. During this process, PDA is prone to non-covalent aggregate via π-π stacking ad hydrogen interactions. Thus, PDA coatings normally are uneven, and large PDA aggregates exist on the deposited surface.

Enhancing the polymerization and deposition rate of dopamine has been regarded as an effective solution to address the above issues. For fast PDA deposition, several agents, e.g., NaIO4 [19], Fe3+/H2O2 [21], and amine [22], or even ultraviolet light [23] have been introduced to accelerate the deposition rate of PDA based on the mechanism of dopamine oxidation. The coating time can be remarkably shortened from hours to a few minutes. Apart from deposition time, the homogeneous morphology of PDA coating is also crucial. When strong oxidizers are applied such as ammonium persulfate, large PDA aggregates will be formed in a short time, which is not beneficial to architect a defect-free surface [24]. Considering the above two factors, Zhang et al. constructed a uniform PDA coating triggered by the CuSO4/H2O2 agents, where abundant reactive oxygen species (ROS) were produced to facilitate the polymerization of dopamine in 45 min [25].

Inspired by the successful exploration in the promotion of dopamine polymerization by ROS, a new strategy is proposed to promote the fast deposition of PDA by adopting carbon quantum dots (CQDs) as a trigger. CQDs have aroused much interest because they feature excellent optical properties, good biocompatibility, good water solubility, low cost, and simple fabrication and modification. Meanwhile, as a new kind of zero-dimensional nanomaterial, CQDs manifest high surface area and easy synthesis [26,27]. More importantly, CQDs can act as electron acceptors, demonstrating their potential to perform pro-oxidation [28,29]. This behavior has important effects on the formation of ROS by CQDs. The ROS-producing activities of CQDs under illumination have been proved by several studies [30,31], especially, CQDs doped with nitrogen or functionalized with an amino group have a superior ability to generate ROS [32]. CQDs display the advantage of easily tuning the intrinsic ROS-regulating properties over other ROS regulators. Benefiting from the coexistence of the excitonic and charge-carrier, energy-/electron-transfer mediated photochemical reactions are conducted under appropriate light irradiation to generate large amounts of ROS [33].

In this work, nitrogen-contained CQDs were synthesized via pyrolysis of lysine. The resultant CQDs could generate ROS under visible light irradiation, further successfully enhancing the polymerization and deposition of dopamine. The process of dopamine oxidation by CQDs was analyzed through UV–vis spectroscopy. Further, PDA-coated NF membranes were prepared through this fast deposition strategy. Through the XPS analyses, the chemical structures of formed PDA coating were discussed. The PDA coating exhibited uniform surface morphology. Finally, the separation performances of the resultant NF membrane were estimated through dye/salt separation. This strategy offered an efficient and environmentally friendly PDA deposition method and is of vital importance for displaying the performance of PDA in NF membranes.

Polyacrylonitrile substrates (PAN, molecular weight cutoff of 50000 Da) were purchased from Zhongke Ruiyang Membrane Technology Co., Ltd. Lysine, dopamine, tris (hydroxymethyl) aminomethane (Tris), 2’ 7’-dichlorodihydroflurescein diacetate (DCFH-DA), methyl blue (MYB, MW=799.8 Da), congo red (CR, MW=696 Da), methylene blue (MB, MW=319 Da), sodium sulfate (Na2SO4), sodium chloride (NaCl), sodium hydroxide (NaOH), and polyethylene glycols (PEGs) with the molecular weights were all obtained from Shanghai Aladdin Reagent Co., Ltd. Home-made ultrapure water was used in all experiments.

In a typical process, lysine was heated at 220 oC for 1 h as reported in the literature [34]. After cooling in air to room temperature, the resulting solid residue was dissolved in 20 mL of pure water and sonicated for 30 min. Then the CQD solution was filtered with filter membranes (0.22 µm) to remove large particles. After dialysis, the solid CQDs were obtained by freeze-drying for 24 h.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fast PDA deposition promoted by CQDs on PAN substrate
ce:section.ce:para: The hydrolyzed PAN was prepared through hydrolysis in a 2 M NaOH solution at 50 oC for 30 min. Then, the hydrolyzed PAN was rinsed with water until pH 7.

The NF membranes were obtained via fast deposition of dopamine triggered by CQDs under visible light irradiation, as illustrated in Fig. 1. The visible light was produced by a xenon lamp (PLS-SXE-300 W) equipped with a 420 nm cut-off filter. First, the PAN substrate was fixed in a customized round holder. Dopamine (2 mg mL-1) with different CQDs contents (0, 2, and 4 mg mL-1, respectively) was completely dissolved in 20 mM Tris aqueous solution (30 mL, pH 8.5). Subsequently, the solutions were quickly poured into the holder to contact the surface of PAN under visible light irradiation. After the deposition, the membranes were washed with pure water followed by drying at 40 oC for 20 min. The resultant NF membranes were named CNFM-X, where X represented the deposition time, i.e., 30, 45, and 60 min), respectively. For a comparative study, the PDA-deposited NF membrane triggered by air was named NFM.

ce:section.ce:section-title: UV–vis spectra of dopamine solutions accelerated by CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The UV–vis absorption for dopamine solutions containing 2 mg mL-1 CQDs was measured to characterize the oxidative polymerization process. The absorption of dopamine solution without CQDs was also tested for comparison to confirm the role of CQDs in dopamine polymerization. The experiment of UV–vis absorption was determined by UV–vis spectrometer (UV-2600) at the wavelengths of 420 nm, which corresponds to indole structure during dopamine polymerization.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Estimation of ROS generated by CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: DFCH-DA was first hydrolyzed with NaOH solution (0.01 M) for 30 min in the dark to obtain DCFH solution. The solution of DCFH was mixed with CQDs and then irradiated by light. To monitor the ROS, the fluorescence intensity at 520 nm was monitored.

Scanning electron microscopy (SEM, GeminiSEM500) was adopted to visualize the surface morphologies of CNFM-X. The surface roughness was measured by atom force microscopy (AFM, XE-100E) at ambient conditions. The chemical and element compositions were characterized by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Thermo Fisher Scientific). The hydrophilicity of membrane surface was analyzed by the contract-angle system (Kruess) at 25 oC.

The perm-selectivity of CNFM-X was evaluated by cross-flow NF equipment at 0.6 MPa. 0.1 g L-1 dye aqueous solutions were used as feed solutions. The effective surface area of test CNFM-X was 22.5 cm2. Before the test, CNFM-X was pre-compacted at 0.6 MPa for 1 h. The water flux (J) and salt rejection (R) were counted by Eqs. 1–2.
(1)
J =
V
At
(2)
R
%
=
C
f
−
C
p
C
f
× 100
Where A (m2) represented the effective area, V (L) was the volume of permeate over a time interval t (h), C
f and C
p were the concentrations of the feed and permeate solution, respectively. The concentrations of dye were determined by UV–vis spectrophotometer, while the salt concentrations were measured by an electrical conductivity meter (Mettler Toledo FE-30).

Highly carcinogenic MYB commonly exists in textile wastewater and poses threat to the environment without effective treatment. Thus, the simulated dyeing wastewater containing MYB (0.1 g L-1) and NaCl (0–10 g L-1) was filtrated to further assess the dye/salt separation ability of CNFM-X. All fluxes and solute rejections were obtained from averages of three repeated tests.

The pore size distribution of membranes was confirmed as follows: The NF membrane was filtrated with 1 g L-1 PEG aqueous solution, then the concentrations between feed solution and permeation were measured by a total organic carbon analyzer (SHIMADZU, Japan). Mean effective pore radius (μp) of the membrane is assumed to the solute geometric radius (μp) when rejection= 50%. The geometric standard deviation of the membrane (σp) is assumed to be the ratio of Stoke radius when rejection = 84.13% to that when rejection = 50%. The membrane pore radius probability density function can be calculated as Eq. 3:
(3)

p
)
2
where rp are the Stokes radii of organic solutes.

To evaluate the antifouling performance of CNFM, the antifouling test was conducted using MYB/NaCl mixture solution. Specifically, CNFM was filtrated with pure water for 2 h, the water flux was denoted as J0. Afterward, the feed solution containing 0.1 g L-1 MYB and 1 g L-1 NaCl was adopted, and the water flux was measured as Jf. Then the fouled CNFM was washed with pure water for 2 h to regenerate the membrane, the water flux was denoted as J1. The above procedures were repeated for 2.5 cycles. Some index, flux recovery ratio (FRR), reversible flux recovery ratio (Fr), and irreversible flux recovery ratio (Fir) were calculated by the Equations:
(4)
FRR =
J
1

× 100 %

−
J
1
J
0
× 100 %
Generally, higher FRR and lower Fir demonstrate the superior antifouling performance of NF membrane.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Accelerated oxidative polymerization of dopamine by CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The successful preparation of CQDs were confirmed by FTIR, XPS, TEM, and UV–vis adsorption spectrum (Fig. S1). Then the promotion effect of CQDs on dopamine polymerization was analyzed. 5, 6-dihydroxyindole unit and its derivatives are the basic building blocks of PDA [35,36]. Thus, the changes in the UV–vis absorption of indole groups (420 nm) were monitored to characterize the polymerization process of dopamine [25]. The color variations and UV–vis absorbance with reaction time were displayed in Fig. 2. The dopamine solution with CQDs turned dark black within 40 min, meanwhile, it showed a darker solution color compared with that of dopamine solution, indicating the rapid generations of PDA triggered by CQDs. The absorbance of dopamine with CQDs solution at the wavelength of 420 nm showed a sharp linear increase. By contrast, the dopamine solution without CQDs showed a slow increase in absorbance. Specifically, the absorbance of dopamine solution with CQDs increased to 3.02 at 60 min, which was much higher than that of dopamine solution oxidated by air (0.69). It was worth noting that although the solution appeared dark black, there was no precipitation in the solution. Besides, no obvious difference in the absorbance of dopamine with or without visible light was found. These results suggested that the CQDs greatly accelerated the oxidation of dopamine in the presence of visible light, which favored the rapid deposition of PDA on the PAN substrate.

For oxidative polymerization, the key element is the generation of sufficient ROS. Thus, we have investigated the photo-induced ROS production capability of CQDs using DCFH assay. The UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectrum of solids CQDs was shown in Fig. 3a, the optical band gap of CQDs could be derived from the Tauc Plot function with a value of 2.07 eV, which enabled the CQDs to be photoactive by visible light [37]. Thus, the CQDs are susceptible to producing electron-hole pairs under visible light irradiation, resulting in the generation of ROS. ROS can oxidize DCFH to strong fluorescent 2’ 7’-dichlorofluorescein (DCF). The fluorescence intensity at 520 nm can be used as an indicator of ROS production capacity [38]. The more ROS are produced, the greater the fluorescence intensity is observed. As depicted in Fig. 3b, the highest increase in ROS was obtained for CQDs under visible light illumination. In the beginning, the solution with CQDs was strongly fluorescent, and we observed a 5.8-fold increase in ROS for CQDs compared to that without CQD (The fluorescence of the blank sample may be due to the oxidation by air during the test, furthermore, the intensity of fluorescence exhibited only small changes) at 5 min. The photoinduced ROS production suggests CQDs as potential triggers for the rapid polymerization of PDA. In this process, CQDs produce a large amount of ROS and act as an oxidant to trigger fast dopamine polymerization.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Deposition of PDA on PAN substrate assisted by CQDs

ce:section.ce:para:
The chemical structures of PDA-deposited membranes were confirmed by ATR-FTIR. The peaks about 3370 cm -1, and 2932 cm-1 corresponding to the stretching vibrations of amine and –OH groups, and –CH2- groups in PDA were observed in all characterized membranes [39,40]. As shown in
Fig. 4a, compared with PDA coating triggered by air, the PDA coating triggered by CQDs (i.e., CNFM-30, CNFM-45, and CNFM-60) showed stronger characteristic adsorption peaks at 1558 cm-1, which was attributed to CC bonds from the indole structure of PDA [41]. Meanwhile, the peak at 1509 cm−1 (N − H scissoring vibrations) exhibited a reduced peak intensity, indicating that PDA incurred intramolecular cyclization and formed indole structures which led to the decreased content of the amino group [42]. The surface chemical compositions of membranes at various deposition time intervals were characterized by XPS to elucidate the evolution behavior of the PDA layer. As indicated in Fig. 4b, all tested membranes exhibited the characteristic peaks for carbon (C1s), nitrogen (N1s), and oxygen (O1s) elements. Different from the PAN substrate, the PDA deposited membranes showed a higher O content but a lower N content, which was mainly because dopamine, as a monomer for polymerization, has a high percentage of O element [21]. The content of O element on CNFM-45 rose from 7.05% to 8.43%, while the content of N element decreased to 17.40%. Further extended the deposition time, the content of O element boosted to 8.90%. Meanwhile, the O/N ratio increased from 0.38 to 0.52, indicating the successful formation of the PDA layer [24]. To further analyze the chemical structure of PDA coatings, the high-resolution C1s spectra of PDA coating triggered by air and CQDs (Fig. 4c-d), were deconvoluted into CO (287.7 eV), C-O/C-N (286.0 eV), and C-C/C-H (284.8 eV) peaks[43,44], respectively. The results showed that, compared with PDA triggered by air, the PDA coating triggered by CQDs exhibited lower C-C/C-H content, and the low content of C-H implied that cyclization and covalent connection played an important role in the PDA coating formation triggered by CQDs [25]. Thus, we can infer that the rapid polymerization of dopamine is realized in the presence of CQD and the formed PDA possesses a high covalent bonding component.

To further investigate the effect of CQDs, the surface morphologies of the PAN and PDA deposited NF membranes were visualized by SEM. A porous and smooth PAN surface morphology was observed. In the case of air oxidization, PDA aggregates were visible on the PAN surface, and the roughness of NFM was 17.24 ± 1.44 nm. However, no obvious visible pores were found on the surface of CNFM, potentially enhancing the solute rejection. Besides, the CNFM also displayed a smooth surface with a low roughness of 12.13 ± 1.33 nm. The results mean the PDA triggered by CQDs can fully cover the PAN surface forming the smooth and integral coating layer [25]. Air-oxidized dopamine has a slow oxidative polymerization rate, and this gradual process is not sufficient to produce enough PDA nanoparticles to fully cover the PAN surface, resulting in poor uniformity of the PDA coating. Besides, the PDA nanoparticles will aggregate into larger particles, resulting in increased roughness of the PDA coating [45], which is not conducive to the formation of a homogeneous PDA coating. In terms of PDA coating triggered by CQDs, the PDA coatings triggered by CQDs show high uniformity, which is ascribed to the rapid polymerization of dopamine and the homogenous nucleation followed by the quick deposition of PDA nanoparticles [25]. Besides, it has been proved that PDA incur intramolecular cyclization and form indole structure which is beneficial to the homogeneity of structure [46]. As a result, uniform and compact PDA coatings are formed.

The thickness of the membrane was characterized by SEM cross-section image, and the results were shown in Fig. 6. The hydrolyzed PAN membrane had an asymmetric structure with a dense skin layer whose thickness was about 169 nm. After PDA deposition, the thickness of NFM, CNFM-45, and CNFM-60 were 33, 27.5, and 50.7 nm, respectively. The thickness of PDA deposition layer triggered by CQDs was higher than that triggered by air. The results indicated that CQDs could promote the polymerization and deposition of dopamine.

The surface hydrophilicity of PAN, NFM, and CNFM-X were evaluated by water contact angle (WCA) measurements ( Fig. 7). The PAN featured a highly hydrophilic surface with a WCA of 36.9 oC, which was ascribed to the hydrophilic carboxyl groups, as well as porous structures on the PAN surface [47]. All membranes deposited by PDA exhibited an increased WCA. The WCA of NFM was similar to that of PAN substrate, probably because the PDA triggered by air was not fully cover the PAN surface. As shown in Fig. 5, some pores and PDA aggregates could be found on the surface of NFM. As for CNFM, uniform PDA coating triggered by CQDs was formed on PAN substrate and covered highly hydrophilic carboxyl groups, which resulted in a less hydrophilic membrane surface. Additionally, the aromatic rings in the PDA structure also limited the hydrophilization effect [48]. Above all, we could infer that CQDs promoted uniform and rapid deposition of PDA, therefore significantly altering the surface properties of PAN substrate.

CNFM-60 possessed a smaller membrane pore size (μp) and narrower pore size distribution (σp) compared with NFM ( Fig. 8a). The decreased pore size of CNFM-60 demonstrated the formation of a denser and more compact membrane. This was mainly attributed to CQDs used which produced ROS to facilitate more PDA that deposited onto the PAN substrate under the same deposition time. Hydrolyzed PAN was negatively charged as shown in Fig. 8b. After PDA was deposited on the PAN substrate, the surface zeta potentials of NFM and CNFM-60 were both decreased but still negatively charged, which phenomenon was also reported previously [21,49].

The NF separation performance of CNFM was measured with various preparation conditions, e.g., deposition time and mass ratio of DA: CQDs ( Fig. 9). As displayed in Fig. 9a-b, compared with NFM, the CQDs triggered PDA coating membrane (i.e., CNFM) showed a much higher dye rejection under the same deposition time. It showed that the addition of CQDs in dopamine solution was conducive to forming the less defective separation NF membranes. Moreover, the separation performance of CNFM could be tuned by different deposition times. With the longer deposition time, the MYB rejection significantly increased from 88.9% to 99.7%, while the water flux slowly decreased from 31.48 to 24.4 L m-2 h-1. In terms of NFM, even though the deposition time was 60 min, the MYB rejection was only increased to 54.1%, failing to meet the requirements of dye wastewater treatment. This was ascribed to the fact that CQDs had a priority to promote the oxidation of dopamine for polymerization and fast deposition on PAN substrate. As shown in Fig. 8a, CNFM-60 possessed smaller membrane pore size and narrower pore size distribution, indicating a denser and more compact PDA layer compared with NFM. Moreover, the PDA coating triggered by CQDs contained more cyclization and covalent connection of dopamine quinone structures (Fig. 4), providing further proof. Thus, compared with NFM, the increased dye rejection of CNFM-60 was mainly attributed to size exclusion. Three types of dyes, MYB, CR, and MB, were chosen as model organics to further evaluate the effect of DA: CQDs ratios on NF performance. As shown in Fig. 9c-d, no obvious promoted effect of DA: CQDs mass ratio on CNFM separation performance was observed. The increased mass ratio led to a slight water flux improvement and a decreased dye rejection, regardless of MYB, CR, and MB. For instance, when the mass ratio of DA: CQDs was 1:2, the flux slightly increased to 25.08 L m-2 h-1 accompanied by 98.3% MYB rejection. The possible explanation is that 2 mg mL-1 CQDs is sufficient to promote the rapid deposition of dopamine, forming a defect-free selective separation layer, when further increase the content of CQDs, excess CQDs act as nanofiller and reduce the density of the PDA coating [50] (more discussion was presented in Fig. S2). Thus, the flux of the membrane slightly increases and the dye rejection decreases. As such, the optimal membrane preparation conditions: the concentration of CQDs is 2 mg mL-1 and deposition time is 60 min. The resultant CNFM-60 followed dye rejections order MYB (99.7%) > CR (91.8%) > MB (74.7%). MYB with larger molecular weight and higher negative charge ( Table 1) were highly rejected by CNFM-60, compared with CR. This demonstrated the synergistic effect of the Donnan effect and steric hindrance in dye rejections. Small and positive dyes (MB) with sizes of 1.25 * 0.51 nm were also rejected by CNFM-60. The moderate rejections were ascribed to the aggregation of dyes, which was induced by the π-π stacking interaction in aromatic rings [24]. The larger dimension of the dye clusters contributes to the moderate rejection by CNFM-60 correspondingly.

The salt rejections of Na2SO4, MgSO4, MgCl2, and NaCl by CNFM-60 were 26.10%, 21.66%, 16.95%, and 12.6%, respectively, in line with the separation performance of negatively charged NF membranes. It was also proved that the CNFM-60 was a kind of loose NF membrane. Thus, the selectivity separation of CNFM-60 was verified by filtrating MYB/NaCl mixture solution (0.1 g L-1 MYB). Fig. 10a revealed that the water flux and dye rejections of MYB gradually decreased with the increase in NaCl concentration. When the concentration of NaCl was 1 g L-1, the water flux of CNFM-60 was 24.9 L m-2 h-1, and the separation factor of MYB/NaCl was 199.8. Further increased to 10 g L-1, the water flux of CNFM-60 declined to 22.6 L m-2 h-1, and the rejections of MYB and NaCl were 95.8% and 10.7%, respectively. Such deteriorated performance was reasonably ascribed to the serious concentration polarization phenomenon of solutes near the CNFM surface, since high NaCl concentration screens the electrical repulsion between membrane and solutes [53,54]. The CNFM-60 membrane exhibits good MYB rejection (>97%) and low NaCl rejections (<10%) when the concentration of NaCl ranges from 0 to 6 g L-1, demonstrating good separation of dyes and inorganic salts.

The antifouling property of NF membranes is of vital importance for their long-term operation [55,56]. Cyclic fouling experiments were conducted using a simulated solution composed of 0.1 g L-1 MYB and 1 g L-1 NaCl to characterize the antifouling behavior of CNFM-60. Fig. 10b showed the time-dependent normalized water flux variations during MYB/NaCl mixture solution filtration. The water flux of CNFM-60 declined to 69.5% of the original water flux after filtration of MYB/NaCl mixture solution. MYB can adsorb on the membrane surface via hydrophobic interaction and hydrogen bonds, and the addition of NaCl causes concentration polarization [45,57]. Both reasons increase the mass transfer resistance of the membrane, and thus decreased the water flux. After rinsing with pure water for 2 h, the permeation recovered to 89.6% of the original water flux and kept a subtle variation. After 2.5 fouling cycles, the FRR of CNFM-60 was 86.8%, meanwhile, the Fr was 19.5% and the Fir was 13.2%. The existence of 13.2% irreversible flux recovery rate indicated dye adsorption on the membrane surface, however, Fr was higher than Fir, demonstrating flux decline was mainly due to physically reversible fouling. High FRR and Fr both demonstrated the good antifouling performance of CNFM-60 during the separation of MYB/NaCl mixture. Besides, the comprehensive comparisons between CNFM-60 and other reported loose NF membranes in terms of permeability, dye/salt selectivity, and anti-fouling ability were conducted (Table S1), and the results proved that CNFM-60 achieved high dye/salt selectivity and comparable anti-fouling ability. Furthermore, as shown in Fig. 10c, both the water flux and MYB rejection changed little during the long-term test. CNFM-60 membrane showed good stability, which was a vital performance for applications.

A facile strategy to promote polymerization and deposition of PDA on PAN substrate was proposed for preparing efficient dye wastewater treatment NF membranes. CQDs play a crucial role in the acceleration of PDA formation by generating ROS under visible light irradiation. The addition of CQDs shortens the deposition time and is beneficial to forming smooth and uniform PDA coating layers. The surface morphologies of PDA coating triggered by CQDs were confirmed by SEM and AFM characterizations. Furthermore, CQDs enhance the covalent bonding portions of PDA, according to XPS results. Thus, the resultant CNFM-60 exhibits a water flux of 24.4 L m-2 h -1 with high dye rejection (99.7% for MYB). When filtrated with MYB/NaCl mixture solution, CNFM-60 showed high selective separation between dye and salt, as well as good antifouling performance. This work provides a method for the rapid and high-quality deposition of PDA to fully express the advantages of dopamine in membrane surface modification.

Yi-Fang Mi: Conceptualization, Methodology, Supervision, Project administration, Writing - review & editing, Yi-Han Huang: Investigation, Validation, Shu-Heng He：Investigation, Validation, Zhihai Cao：Supervision, Bao-Qing Shentu: Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21908200), the Natural Science Foundation of Zhejiang Province (LY22B060011), and the Zhejiang Provincial Science and Technology Innovation Program (New Young Talent Program) for College Students (2022R406A005).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.colsurfa.2022.130871.
